prompt: Which Turkish letter makes the ‘ch’ sound, as in ‘church’?

explanation.why: The cedilla marks the voiceless sound — ç is ‘ch’, c is ‘j’.

prompt: Which Turkish letter sounds like the English ‘j’ in ‘jam’?

explanation.rule: Turkish c = English ‘j’. (Turkish j is the ‘zh’ of ‘vision’, rare.)

prompt: Which vowel is the DOTLESS i — the ‘e’ sound in ‘open’?

explanation.rule: ı (no dot) is a back vowel; i (dotted) is ‘ee’.
explanation.why: They are different letters AND different sounds — the dot matters.

prompt: Which letter is silent and simply lengthens the vowel before it?

explanation.rule: ğ (yumuşak ge / soft-g) never starts a word and has no sound of its own.
explanation.why: ‘dağ’ is pronounced ‘daa’ — the ğ stretches the a.

prompt: Type the Turkish word for ‘water’ (two letters).

explanation.rule: su — plain s + back rounded u.

prompt: Type the Turkish word for ‘mountain’ — it ends with the silent letter.

explanation.rule: dağ ends in soft-g: silent, lengthens the a.

prompt: Type ‘child’ in Turkish (starts with ç, has two back rounded vowels).

explanation.rule: çocuk = ‘cho-jook’ — ç is ‘ch’, both vowels are o/u (back, rounded).
explanation.why: Practise pairing the ç sound with its letter.

prompt: Type ‘eye’ in Turkish (one syllable, front rounded vowel ö).

explanation.why: ö is front + rounded; it will matter enormously for harmony.

explanation.rule: kız uses the dotless ı — ‘kuhz’, not ‘keez’.
explanation.why: Writing i instead of ı is the classic beginner slip.

explanation.rule: kapı — both vowels back: a, then dotless ı.

explanation.why: Here the i IS dotted — reading the dots correctly cuts both ways.

prompt: Which word contains ONLY front vowels {e i ö ü}?

explanation.rule: Front vowels: e i ö ü. göz has only ö → all-front.
explanation.why: kapı (a, ı), okul (o, u) and çocuk (o, u) are all-back words. This split drives every suffix in the language — next lesson.
